dependencies.notes: orders-service depends on a lot of things. When debugging, start with auth and payments — those are the most common culprits for order failures. Kafka failure degrades event publishing but synchronous order creation still works. S3 failures only affect export feature. Note: orders-db disk usage must be monitored — we have had a WAL archive incident before (INC-4021). Check disk alert thresholds are set.

common_issues.title: Orders failing to create / 500 on POST /orders
symptoms: HTTP 500 on POST /api/v1/orders
Error rate spike on 'Orders Creation' Datadog dashboard
Support tickets: customers completing checkout but no order appearing
orders-service logs showing exception stack traces
likely_causes: payments-service unavailable — orders cannot be placed without payment confirmation
auth-service returning errors — token validation failing
orders-db at capacity (disk full, connection pool exhausted)
inventory-service timeout — stock reservation failing
Kafka producer failing — usually non-blocking but can cause issues in some code paths

steps.action: Check orders-service pod logs for the actual error
steps.command: kubectl logs -l app=orders-service --since=10m | grep -i 'error\|exception\|failed' | tail -30
steps.note: The error message usually tells you exactly which dependency failed. Don't guess — read the logs first.

steps.action: Check all hard dependencies are healthy
steps.command: for svc in auth-service payments-service inventory-service; do echo "=== $svc ==="; kubectl get pods -l app=$svc; done
steps.note: Any CrashLoopBackOff or Pending pods in dependencies will cascade to orders. Fix the dependency first.

steps.action: Check orders-db disk usage
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size('orders'));"
steps.note: If this times out, the DB might already be in trouble. Check the disk alert in Datadog. Disk full = all writes fail silently. This is what happened in INC-4021.

steps.action: Check WAL archive size if disk is high
steps.command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- du -sh /var/lib/postgresql/data/pg_wal/
steps.note: WAL directory growing unexpectedly large suggests the archive cron job is not running. Check cron logs. Last time this happened we had 47 days of unarchived WAL. See INC-4021.

steps.action: If WAL is the problem, prune carefully
steps.command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- pg_archivecleanup /var/lib/postgresql/data/pg_wal/ <last_known_good_wal_file>
steps.note: Do NOT just delete files from pg_wal manually. Use pg_archivecleanup. Get a second set of eyes on this before proceeding. If unsure, escalate to DBA on-call.

steps.action: Check Kafka producer health if dependency services are fine
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- curl -s localhost:8080/internal/kafka/status | jq .
steps.note: Kafka failures should be logged but non-fatal for order creation. If the service is treating Kafka failure as fatal, that's a bug.

common_issues.escalation: Order creation failures are P1 during business hours. Page commerce on-call. If payments-service is the root cause, also page payments on-call.

common_issues.title: Orders stuck in 'Processing' status
symptoms: Orders created but never transition to CONFIRMED or SHIPPED
Kafka consumer lag growing on orders.created topic
Fulfillment emails not being sent
orders-service logs show orders created successfully but downstream silent
likely_causes: Kafka consumer lag — downstream consumer (notifications, fulfillment) is backed up
Poison-pill message blocking a Kafka partition (deserialization failure on one message stops the whole partition)
notifications-service or fulfillment-service is down
Orders not actually missing — just delayed due to consumer lag

steps.action: Check Kafka consumer lag for orders.created topic
steps.command: kubectl exec -it kafka-0 -- kafka-consumer-groups.sh --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --describe --group notifications-service-cg
steps.note: Look at the LAG column. A few hundred is normal. Tens of thousands means something is blocking consumption. Note: there's also a fulfillment consumer group — check that too. Can't remember the exact group name, check with Dave.

steps.action: Check for poison-pill messages
steps.command: kubectl logs -l app=notifications-service | grep -i 'deserializ\|parse\|error' | tail -20
steps.note: A deserialization error repeating on the same offset means the consumer is stuck on a bad message. The message offset will be in the log.

steps.action: Skip the poison-pill message if confirmed
steps.command: kubectl exec -it kafka-0 -- kafka-consumer-groups.sh --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --group notifications-service-cg --topic orders.created --reset-offsets --to-offset <bad_offset+1> --execute
steps.note: Only do this if you have confirmed the message is malformed and cannot be processed. The skipped message goes to the dead-letter queue if DLQ is configured. Check that it's configured first — it was added after INC-4021 but may not be on all consumer groups.

steps.action: Check if orders are actually lost or just delayed
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT id, status, created_at FROM orders WHERE status = 'PROCESSING' AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '30 minutes' LIMIT 20;"
steps.note: Orders more than 30 minutes in PROCESSING state are genuinely stuck. Orders less than 30 minutes old might just be delayed by consumer lag. Don't panic if it's the latter.

steps.action: Manually reprocess stuck orders if needed
steps.command: There's a script for this somewhere — ask Dave. It's not in this runbook yet. TODO: add it.
steps.note: Last time we needed to reprocess, Dave ran it manually. It re-publishes the orders.created events for specific order IDs. Very much a last resort.

common_issues.escalation: If consumer lag exceeds 100,000 messages or orders have been stuck for over an hour, escalate to #commerce-platform and page commerce on-call.

common_issues.title: Inventory going negative / overselling
symptoms: inventory-service shows negative stock count for a SKU
Multiple customers ordered same last item simultaneously
Orders confirmed for items that should have been out of stock
likely_causes: Race condition: check-and-decrement on inventory is not atomic
Inventory reservation call is async or being skipped under load
Cache serving stale stock count — Redis cache not invalidated on purchase

steps.action: Identify affected SKUs
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/inventory-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT sku_id, stock_count FROM inventory WHERE stock_count < 0;"
steps.note: Get the list of negative-stock SKUs. Cross-reference with orders to understand how many customers are affected.

steps.action: Temporarily set affected SKUs to unavailable
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/inventory-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "UPDATE inventory SET available = false WHERE sku_id IN ('<sku_ids>');"
steps.note: This prevents more overselling while you investigate. Coordinate with product team before doing this — they may prefer a different approach.

steps.action: Check if inventory reservation is using atomic operations
steps.command: Look at inventory-service source code, reservation handler. It should use a SELECT FOR UPDATE or equivalent. If not, that's the bug.
steps.note: This is a code review task, not a kubectl task. The fix requires a deploy. In the meantime, scaling inventory-service down to 1 pod reduces (but doesn't eliminate) the race.

steps.action: Decide what to do with oversold orders
steps.command: N/A — this is a business decision, not a technical one.
steps.note: Options: (1) honour all orders and fulfil from back-stock/reorder. (2) Cancel excess orders with apology and voucher. (3) Something else. Get Dave and product lead on a call.

common_issues.escalation: Overselling is a business-impacting incident. Involve commerce on-call and product lead immediately. Do not make unilateral decisions about order cancellations.

common_issues.title: Duplicate orders created on payment retry
symptoms: Customer reports being shipped two identical orders
orders-db shows two orders with same cart session but different order IDs
Stripe shows one successful charge but two fulfillment events
likely_causes: Idempotency token missing on order creation endpoint
Frontend retried after timeout and created second order with new idempotency token
Payment retry logic creating new order instead of resuming existing one

steps.action: Confirm duplication in orders-db
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT cart_session_id, count(*), array_agg(id) FROM orders GROUP BY cart_session_id HAVING count(*) > 1 ORDER BY count(*) DESC LIMIT 10;"
steps.note: If you see multiple orders per cart_session_id, that's your duplicate. Check the timestamps — usually within seconds of each other.

steps.action: Cancel the duplicate orders
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "UPDATE orders SET status = 'CANCELLED', cancellation_reason = 'duplicate' WHERE id = '<duplicate_order_id>';"
steps.note: Cancel the later one unless there's a reason to prefer it. Make sure any shipped items are intercepted if possible — coordinate with fulfillment.

steps.action: Check if idempotency is enforced on order creation
steps.command: kubectl logs deploy/orders-service | grep 'idempotency\|cart_session' | tail -20
steps.note: Should see idempotency key logged for each order creation. If absent, the feature may not be deployed or is misconfigured.

steps.action: Check current idempotency config
steps.command: kubectl get deploy/orders-service -o json | jq '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].env[] | select(.name | contains("IDEMPOTENCY"))'
steps.note: ORDER_IDEMPOTENCY_ENABLED should be true. If missing, feature is off. Was added as a fix post-INC-3844 — should be present in v4.14.0+.

common_issues.escalation: If more than 5 customers affected, notify customer support lead to prepare outreach. Notify Dave.

common_issues.title: Order data visible across tenants / privacy leak
symptoms: Customer reports seeing another customer's orders
Admin export contains orders from all tenants
GET /orders returns more results than expected for a user
likely_causes: Missing tenant_id filter in query (most likely after a migration or new endpoint was added)
Caching layer not scoped to user/tenant context
Admin export endpoint missing tenancy clause

steps.action: STOP — do not investigate casually. This is a data privacy incident.
steps.command: N/A
steps.note: Immediately notify security-team@example.com and alice@example.com. Document what you know. Do not access data you're not authorised to access during investigation.

steps.action: Disable the affected endpoint immediately
steps.command: kubectl set env deployment/orders-service ORDER_EXPORT_ENABLED=false
steps.note: Or set a feature flag to disable. The priority is stopping data exposure, not understanding it.

steps.action: Identify how long the leak has been active
steps.command: kubectl logs deploy/orders-service --since=24h | grep '<affected_endpoint>' | wc -l
steps.note: You need to know the window of exposure for GDPR notification purposes. Preserve logs — do not allow them to rotate.

steps.action: Identify which customers' data was exposed
steps.command: Access logs from the affected endpoint. Correlate requesting user with order data returned. This will require a forensic query — get DBA on-call involved.
steps.note: This is for GDPR breach notification. Legal needs to know within 72 hours if this is a reportable breach. Do not delay.

common_issues.escalation: This is a P0 security incident. Page security on-call, notify Dave, and alert legal team. Do not discuss in public Slack channels.

common_issues.title: Slow order queries / dashboard timeouts
symptoms: Admin dashboard timing out when loading order lists
GET /orders endpoint P99 > 5 seconds
orders-db CPU spiking to 90%+ during dashboard load
Postgres slow query log filling up
likely_causes: Missing index on frequently queried columns (status, created_at, customer_id)
Analytics dashboard firing too many queries simultaneously
A new query added without EXPLAIN ANALYZE check
Table bloat from high write volume not being VACUUMed

steps.action: Identify slow queries
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT query, calls, mean_exec_time FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC LIMIT 10;"
steps.note: pg_stat_statements must be enabled. If you get 'relation not found', it's not enabled — ask DBA. Alternatively check the slow query log: kubectl exec orders-db-primary-0 -- tail -100 /var/log/postgresql/postgresql.log

steps.action: Check indexes on orders table
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT indexname, indexdef FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename = 'orders';"
steps.note: Should have indexes on: customer_id, created_at, status, and ideally composite (customer_id, created_at DESC). If the composite index is missing, add it — but use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY to avoid locking.

steps.action: Add missing index without locking
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_orders_customer_created ON orders(customer_id, created_at DESC);"
steps.note: CONCURRENTLY means it doesn't lock the table. Takes longer but safe for production. Monitor orders-db CPU while it runs.

steps.action: If analytics dashboard is the culprit, disable or rate-limit it
steps.command: kubectl set env deployment/orders-service ANALYTICS_DASHBOARD_ENABLED=false
steps.note: The analytics dashboard queries are known to be expensive. There's a ticket to rewrite them against a materialized view but it hasn't been done yet. See JIRA COM-512.

common_issues.escalation: Slow queries causing customer-facing timeouts are P2. If affecting all users, escalate to P1 and page commerce on-call.

general_health_checks: kubectl get pods -l app=orders-service — all pods Running
Check Datadog: 'Orders Overview' — order creation success rate, p99 latency
Check Kafka consumer lag: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group notifications-service-cg
Check orders-db disk usage — alert should fire at 75%, check manually if near that
Check pg_wal directory size on orders-db-primary-0 — should not be growing unbounded
known_gaps: Manual order reprocessing script exists but is not in this runbook — ask Dave
Analytics dashboard query performance is known bad — JIRA COM-512 not yet resolved
Inventory reservation is not fully atomic — race condition exists under high concurrency
Consumer group names for fulfillment service not documented — check with Dave
Order export feature streams rows but very large exports (10M+ orders) have not been tested
notes: Orders depends on nearly everything. When in doubt, check your dependencies before assuming orders-service itself is broken. The dependency health check script (check_deps.sh) may or may not be up to date — verify before trusting it.
